chapter_summary: The Astika legend (Adi Parva). At Naimisha forest, Shaunaka asks Sauti why King Janamejaya undertook the snake-sacrifice (sarpasatra) to destroy all serpents, and why Astika — foremost of those who recite mantras — rescued the serpents from the blazing fire; he also asks whose son the king and the brahmana each were. Sauti answers that this ancient itihasa was composed by Krishna-Dvaipayana (Vyasa) and first told to the brahmanas by Sauti's own father Lomaharshana, Vyasa's wise pupil. Astika's father was the great ascetic Jaratkaru of the Yayavara line — a lifelong celibate (urdhvareta), eater of restraint, fixed in fierce austerity. Wandering, Jaratkaru saw his own forefathers (the Pitris) hanging head-down in a great pit, clinging to a single tuft of virana grass whose root a hidden rat was gnawing away. They told him they were sinking because their line faced extinction: their sole surviving descendant, a foolish Jaratkaru, refused to take a wife. Learning he himself was that descendant, Jaratkaru vowed to wed — but only a maiden bearing his own name, given to him freely as alms, and on his own terms. Searching the earth he found none, until in a forest he cried aloud thrice for a bride. Vasuki, lord of serpents, came forward offering his sister, also named Jaratkaru, whom he had guarded for this purpose; the marriage neutralized the snake-mother's curse that the serpents would be burned in Janamejaya's sacrifice. They wed by due rite, and a son, Astika, was born — an ascetic, master of the Vedas and Vedangas, who later freed the serpents at the sacrifice and delivered his maternal kin and his father's ancestors. Having begotten Astika and gained supreme dharma, Jaratkaru in time ascended to heaven with his forefathers.

story.title: The Birth of Astika and the Vow of Jaratkaru
story.arc: A frame-question opens the tale: why did Janamejaya try to burn all serpents, and why did Astika save them? The answer reaches back a generation. The celibate ascetic Jaratkaru, refusing marriage, discovers his own ancestors hanging head-down over a pit, their support-root gnawed by a rat — a vivid image of a lineage about to fall for want of a descendant. Moved, he sets an almost impossible condition: he will marry only a maiden of his own name, freely given. The serpent-king Vasuki, needing exactly this union to lift his clan's death-curse, presents his sister Jaratkaru. The marriage joins ascetic and serpent worlds; their son Astika is born to be the deliverer who will halt the snake-sacrifice and redeem both his father's ancestors and his mother's people. Jaratkaru, his debt to the Pitris paid, ascends to heaven.
story.timeline_note: Set in the Naimisha forest as a recitation by Sauti; the embedded events (Jaratkaru's marriage, Astika's birth) precede Janamejaya's snake-sacrifice, which the surrounding Adi Parva will narrate in full.
story.comic_potential: Strong visual beats: the ancestors dangling upside-down in a pit clutching a single grass-root while a hidden rat nibbles it; the lone ascetic crying out thrice in an empty forest for any bride at all; the dramatic entrance of the serpent-king offering a sister who happens to share the exact required name.

teachings.teaching: Spiritual austerity alone does not discharge one's debt to the ancestors; the Pitris tell Jaratkaru that neither the fruits of dharma nor accumulated tapas win the state attained by those who leave offspring — continuation of the line is itself a dharma owed to one's forebears.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya framework, the 'ancestors' and 'lineage' read inwardly as the unbroken transmission of life-force: the urdhvareta yogi who turns the seed upward must still ensure the energy fructifies and is passed on rather than merely hoarded, or the very current that sustains him stagnates — here dramatized as the root being gnawed away.

teachings.teaching: A vow may be kept on exacting terms without abandoning it: Jaratkaru consents to marry only a maiden of his own name, given as alms, on his own conditions — honoring both his renunciate discipline and his duty to his ancestors at once.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma reads such conditional surrender as the disciplined will of the practitioner — yielding to necessity (here, progeny) yet never loosening the inner rule (the same-name, alms-given condition), so that engagement with the world remains an act of controlled sadhana, not of slackening.

teachings.teaching: One supremely qualified soul can redeem an entire web of kin: Astika, master of the Vedas and Vedangas, delivers the serpents, his maternal uncles, his other relations, and his paternal ancestors, while gratifying gods and rishis — a single liberated being clears debts owed across many.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's view this prefigures the realized yogi whose attainment is not private: the awakened consciousness, by its very completeness, lifts those connected to it — ancestral, familial and cosmic — echoing the Kriya idea that one accomplished practitioner uplifts the line.
